speaker: interviewer
text: That-- because Auschwitz seemed to sort of come and go very quickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, I know that. You--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I understand the logistics of it. So that would-- that would be my thing, you know, like the sort of different day-to-day things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I found it very hard to get that out of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll-- we'll go back to when you-- wait one minute, until--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do You want to ID it now?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I just-- sorry, I'm just going to kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, one time when came airplanes, then we started outside to dance.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE] Are you ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to just ID it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, just a minute. Take number three. This is Sarah Salamon. We'd like-- we'll continue with Auschwitz now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- you were saying about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, came the airplanes, and they started to bomb Auschwitz and threw for us all letters. 'Not too long, you will be free.' This give us a little bit, you know, power.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the language that the letters were written in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that the-- they came-- they came from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. Polish, German, 'You-- you will not suffer long. Any day, any minute. Be strong.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say it gave you power. What kind of power did it give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hope. We-- you know, there is a word in Polish-- [SPEAKING POLISH]. Our people, when they go in the water and he can't go out, is anything if he can catch, he grab it. He wanted to help himself. This, we got. the-- this-- this hope that somebody care about us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What reaction did the guards have to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they shoot-- they wanted to shoot us. [SPEAKING GERMAN] Go in in the barrack! Yeah. And at last, they threw away the-- the pistols and everything, and they run away, the last-- the-- the last weeks, from Auschwitz. Very little was left in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were-- when you were in Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you were in the barrack with Yoli--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what kind of things happened throughout the day? Can you tell us about, other than when you were being interrogated by the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, there's always something what one is jealous of the other one. Because I was typing. Anytime I got, till Dr. [? Bimko ?] took me [NON-ENGLISH]. Then, I came just in the night to [? Yolka ?] to sleep there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was very ugly people between-- you know, because there was from every country somebody. And the worst was from [NON-ENGLISH]. You know? They hate the Polish Jews. And, I-- I-- I don't know. These where other prisoners? Are you talking--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish prisoners, or not Jewish prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish-- yeah. The [NON-ENGLISH], horrible people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But the most important thing, we survived. But I-- but sometime, I think, mine god, what I-- what I gained from this surviving? Not a child. Not a family. You are alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say not-- your father. What happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. Mine father and my husband, they was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see them there?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I hear about them. They was like political, you know? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Hitler told one thing. I will first kill the intelligent, because the rest is like cowards and pigs. They will-- they-- they will kill themself. He got one mind that shouldn't be somebody what used brains. There was a underground organization what we got some, you know, news every time, papers they delivered us of the block. This hold us a little bit. We got [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wait for something better. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But you know, no paper and no pencil can describe this. This is out of the question.

speaker: interviewer
text: If-- if we go back to-- you were in Mauthausen as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were there for about how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long, about a couple, three or four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- you were guarded by whom in Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who were your guards in Mauthausen? Were they German?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. Sure. Sure, German. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But you know what? Every organization sent parcels, food, except Jewish people. They didn't receive nothing. And this was such a ugly thing to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that was on purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Why on purpose? To kill? They kill enough. 7 million people they kill.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, one night, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] for millions, I wouldn't wanted to hear the praying from crematorium. From the fire, it goes in the-- and-- and-- and you-- you hear it, every time. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] God, where are you? Where is the promises? Why? I-- I'm a religious man. I believe in you. And this, you know, you never forget in your life. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Like once I came, I went here, I met this-- through a butcher store to buy something. And this was a couple [NON-ENGLISH] You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]. And we did talk. Oh, you were-- you were from the green. I says, I am from the blue, not from the green. And she says, oh, we got very bad times here, too, in the war. We didn't got steaks. They all send it to the soldiers. Oh. And I-- and I was mad. I says, [INAUDIBLE]. You didn't got the-- but you got other meat-- chicken, duck, goose. You-- you-- you wasn't hungry, like we was hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- this happened when you first came here? When-- when I came-- yeah. The-- can-- we'll-- we'll come to that. When-- when you-- when you were at Mauthausen for-- for about four weeks, then you went from there to where, from Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit walking, and a little bit we got trucks on the way. You know, some soldiers drived. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Not too much, really. We went out from Auschwitz, a couple thousand. But they came of the place to Bergen-Belsen, not much. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in Bergen-Belsen for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Bergen-Belsen like-- what we have now, what month?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, it's January.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's still January? February, we came. Soon, the middle February. In April, we was free.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what they did? They put in poison in the flour what they bake bread. Who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS. They saw the-- the last the war. And one from the [NON-ENGLISH], from the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], you know, one tell the other, and just slowly by slowly, put the news to the camp that nobody should eat bread. We were so hungry. And there was whole-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how should I say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Stacks? Piles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Piles bread. And everybody bite their finger and didn't touch it. Because life is very expensive, you know? And then, the baker started to bake bread out the SS men. And they went-- the day what came in the English troops to Bergen-Belsen, they came Jewish Meyor, Eric-- I remember his name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they said, children, you are all free. [SPEAKING GERMAN] He went down, put in [NON-ENGLISH]. And he started to sing the [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know if I saw so many people crying, dancing, and go, do anything with the SS what you wanted. I says, I wouldn't touch it. This is [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the guards that were left? Were there any guards left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah! Sure! I don't know-- a-- a lot have to clean up the place, because people got durchfall. You know durchfall? Diarrhea. They didn't got what to eat, they eat all [NON-ENGLISH]. And then, when the soldiers came in, they got cans, the fat. And they didn't know nothing about what the-- and they give them the-- the-- the cans they should eat. And this make-- thousands and thousands of people died because of this. Then, I put together healthy boys what we found, and a couple woman, and went in in the SS kitchen. And I says to Eric, to this Meyor, [? Eric, ?] I need rice and cocoa, nothing more. And we cooked day and night rice and cocoa to drink. And this stopped the diarrhea. But over 10,000 people pass away. You know, you have-- in such a situation, you have to use you brains a little bit. I says, Eric, stop to give the soldier cans, because there will not be one person alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: The prisoners who are left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: They couldn't cope with the food that was given.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thanks to God for this, I can say that a lot I have to do with it. Day and night, I cooked. Day and night, and served to drink. Cocoa, no sugar, nothing, and rice with water, that's all-- a little bit salt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- the English stayed with you for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was a long time. We-- in '47, Benny mine is born. Benny is born in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Benny's your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I got married in Bergen-Belsen with Dave.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your-- your-- what was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that your first husband was dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I knew it. One Polish officer was with him together in Auschwitz. And he- he came-- he looked after me. You know, one of the others, he says, Mrs. Salamon, your husband is dead and you father. They was shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why?

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in Bergen-Belsen, what kind of things were you doing? Were you working as a nurse then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I worked in the hospital, but then I got pregnant. In the meantime, I received from Vienna a letter from university that I can come and continue mine education. And I was already-- I-- I don't know-- four or five months pregnant. Then I cried, day and night. And my husband says, don't cry. When I will need a doctor, I will know where to find him. Now, I need a wife. This was his words.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't go to Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't. What? I will go, a pregnant woman? Then I need a nursery. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, when I got Benny, then I says to mine husband, now, let me go. It's just two years to finish. He says, and who will watch the child? Should I lose now-- is not enough that I lost two children? Now, should I lose this only child? But he lost it, anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it-- I worked to give him education. Everyone in Toronto know how I work, how many hours. Two shifts, I work in hospital. Came home at 11:15, past 11:00, and started to do something in the house. That boy go to school in the morning. Put him in clean clothes. And how much the-- the payments was, university.

speaker: interviewee
text: The professor-- the professors, they write the books, and they make the students buy the books. You know? Hundred-twenty dollars, hundred-fifty dollars a book. And this, you have because they make you, you know, to flunk. When you don't agree what the professor say, it-- it-- it has to be this book you have to study. You don't win nothing. Before we continue with Benny, can we come back to what-- after the war, you were in Bergen-Belsen with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Bergen-Belsen with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, two years. We-- we-- when Benny was born, we went to Sweden, to Malmo. It's a-- it's beautiful people and a beautiful-- a beautiful country. I don't know now. We are, I think, 45 years in-- in-- in Toronto already.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Leningrad-- or Leninberg? Did you-- you were interviewed after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Leninberg, in Germany. I-- I was a witness, like you-- you know? There is all papers, what I talk in court.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: 35 Germans. There was-- there was 34 SS men in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Nuremberg. A Jewish [? Mayor. ?] You can imagine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I-- when they call me in, if I know this SS man, then I fainted. You know? Then, he came-- he was a doctor, too-- and they-- they-- they-- I don't know-- they took me to the nursery there by the-- by the court. And I was two days lying there. Nurses, doctors, was with me. When I felt better, then I come back. And the [? Mayor ?] says to me-- he came-- he says, what is your name, dear? I says, Sarah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You still remember your Jewish name? I says, sure. Then he says to me, Jewish, in the ear, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. They will be hanged. And there, SS man, what came in the crematorium and take me out, he says, when I take you out from crematorium, why you make me to die? I says, a simple thing-- because you did the same thing to our people. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Didn't you remember, in this moment, when I took you out from crematorium? I says, there was no time to remember this. This was one drop of-- of a-- of a ocean, what you did. You take me out, and another young girl, and 3,000 woman, they was killed in this-- in this time-- 3,000 woman. Then, you can imagine. You were in Nuremberg for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, three weeks I was in court, day and night, with [NON-ENGLISH], the one that I was-- is afraid that they will kill me, you know? When I went from hotel, I wanted to a restroom to go. Then the [NON-ENGLISH] come with me in the restroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of questions were you asked when you were in Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just tell me this what you lived, you saw it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But one day, I couldn't anymore. Then I fainted. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then, the [? Mayor ?] came in when I was in there. There was a nursery there. And he says, with this, what you fainted now, today, you give them all a string of the-- And I went-- they give me a airplane to go to Poland, because I was sure that my son is alive. And I came there, and I saw the old lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Poland. They bring me with the airplane. And the airplane wait for me, because I say I will not be in this city or in-- in this-- the Pollacks was very, very, rough, especially for the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back to Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back, and I went to the old lady. And she got a stand with cigarettes-- because her husband was killed. He was a-- a-- how to say-- a [NON-ENGLISH] in Russia-- airplane, he was killed. Then she got a privilege to sell cigarettes-- a stand. And I went to-- she was already, you know, like she couldn't see good when I came in. And I says, I don't smoke, but I wanted to talk with her if she will recognize me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, may I have a pack of cigarettes? What kind? I says, I don't know what kind, but not strong. And then I-- I says, how much is the cigarettes? Then she told me, that's [NON-ENGLISH]. The cigarette's name was [NON-ENGLISH], Freedom, this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I says, my God, how cheap the freedom is in-- in Poland. And she recognized my voice. And she says, Sasha! She ran out and started to cry and this. You are alive! You are alive! I says, I am alive. But you came a little bit-- a week too late. [? Sammy ?] is not alive. And then she told me how the-- by the-- by our stand, they killed the-- the boy. And she went out, and she bent down to him. She wanted to help him. She call him booby. She-- she-- she says, booby, my mother is alive. They say, he tell her and he closed the eyes. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out what happened to Barbara?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they arrested the priest to-- in-- to Russia. And he was there, like a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in a special camp. And he put her in some nursing home. But who knows who? And I don't know anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I sent-- to the whole world, I sent letters. And once, I got a letter from a girl what-- her name was the same what our name. And her mother's name, just like me, Sophia. And the father, Stanley. And she-- and I-- mine husband says, you want bring her over? I says, I don't know. We'll continue on the-- the next tape. Thank you.
